metadata.description: ACT-focused study guides and free video links aligned to the more ACT-like v2 math bank.
metadata.notes: Video links favor free, well-established channels. Lessons emphasize ACT-style pacing, graphs, tables, and applied multiple-choice questions.

topics.name: Number Sense, Fractions, and Percents
topics.lesson: On ACT Math, these are usually quick points if you convert between fractions, decimals, and percents fast and estimate before computing. Focus on fraction/decimal/percent conversion, order of operations, and quick arithmetic with positive and negative numbers. On ACT-style questions, fluency matters: estimate first, then compute carefully.

videos.title: Order of operations with fractions and exponents

topics.name: Ratios, Proportions, and Unit Rates
topics.lesson: ACT often wraps ratio and rate skills inside word problems, map scales, and unit-price questions. Set up ratios carefully and keep units visible. ACT questions often hide the proportion inside a short word problem, so identify what is being compared before you cross-multiply.

topics.name: Algebraic Expressions and Exponents
topics.lesson: ACT algebra questions here are usually short but reward careful use of exponent rules and equivalent expressions. Practice combining like terms, distributing, and applying exponent rules without overcomplicating. The ACT usually rewards clean symbolic manipulation more than long calculations.

topics.name: Linear Equations
topics.lesson: Expect a mix of direct equations and real-world linear models involving fees, rates, and totals. Keep inverse operations in order and check your solution mentally. ACT linear-equation questions range from simple one-step equations to contextual word problems.

topics.name: Linear Inequalities and Absolute Value
topics.lesson: These questions often test sign changes, interval meaning, and whether you can translate a short condition correctly. Solve inequalities like equations, but remember to reverse the inequality when multiplying or dividing by a negative. For absolute value, interpret distance first, then solve and check.

videos.title: Solving Absolute Value Equations and Inequalities
How To Solve Absolute Value Inequalities

topics.name: Systems of Equations and Modeling
topics.lesson: ACT system questions frequently appear in ticket, mixture, and plan-comparison contexts where setup matters as much as solving. Choose elimination when coefficients line up and substitution when one equation is easy to isolate. In word problems, define variables before writing equations so the model stays organized.

topics.name: Functions and Graphs
topics.lesson: ACT function questions often involve notation, slope, graphs, tables, and picking the right equation from a pattern. Translate comfortably between equations, tables, and graphs. ACT function questions often test evaluation, slope, rate of change, and how transformations affect a graph.

topics.name: Polynomials and Factoring
topics.lesson: These tend to reward pattern recognition: combine like terms, expand efficiently, and spot factorable expressions. Start with the greatest common factor, then look for patterns like trinomials and difference of squares. Factoring is often the fastest path to solving higher-level ACT algebra questions.

topics.name: Quadratic Functions and Equations
topics.lesson: ACT quadratic questions usually focus on factoring, roots, vertex meaning, intercepts, and simple projectile models. Be flexible: solve by factoring, square roots, completing the square, or the quadratic formula depending on the form. Also watch for graph features like vertex, intercepts, and direction of opening.

videos.title: How to use the quadratic formula
Solving quadratic equations using the quadratic formula

topics.name: Rational Expressions and Equations
topics.lesson: These questions mix algebra fluency with domain restrictions, canceled factors, and quick rational equations. Factor first, simplify carefully, and keep excluded values in mind. For rational equations, clear denominators and then check for extraneous solutions.

topics.name: Radicals and Rational Exponents
topics.lesson: ACT uses radicals in both pure algebra and applied equations, so practice simplifying and checking solutions after squaring. Switch comfortably between radical notation and rational exponents. Simplify before solving, and always check radical-equation answers for extraneous solutions.

topics.name: Exponential and Logarithmic Functions
topics.lesson: Exponential questions are more common than deep logarithm work, and ACT usually keeps the algebra short and interpretable. Recognize the structure first: repeated multiplication suggests exponentials, while inverse questions often lead to logs. ACT questions in this area usually emphasize interpretation and simple solving rather than advanced identities.

topics.name: Geometry and Coordinate Geometry
topics.lesson: Expect standard geometry formulas plus coordinate geometry, with many questions tied to diagrams or simple coordinate setups. Know the standard formulas, but also watch for geometry questions that can be solved by reasoning or estimation. Coordinate geometry combines algebra and geometry, so slope, midpoint, and distance should feel automatic.

topics.name: Trigonometry
topics.lesson: ACT trig is usually right-triangle focused: sin, cos, tan, special triangles, and angle-of-elevation applications. Most ACT trig is right-triangle trig plus common unit-circle values. Memorize the basic sine, cosine, and tangent relationships and connect them to triangle side lengths.

topics.name: Statistics, Probability, and Data
topics.lesson: These questions often come from tables, sets, and real-world data summaries, so read carefully before calculating. Read charts carefully and decide whether the task is descriptive statistics, simple probability, or set counting. On ACT-style data questions, the fastest solution usually comes from organizing the information before calculating.
